params.description: Picture graph uses symbols, bar graph uses bars. Visual difference highlighted.

steps.text: You found what's the same about the data. But look closely at how each graph shows it. One uses symbols, one uses bars. They don't look exactly the same.

incorrects.description: Student answered incorrectly. System models the correct answer.

params.description: Title highlights on both graphs, then categories list highlights, then Soccer row/bar highlights on both

steps.text: Let me show you what's the same. Both graphs have the title Favorite Sports. Both show Soccer, Basketball, Baseball, and Tennis. And in both, Soccer has the most with 6. The correct answers are: they have the same title, they show the same categories, and Soccer has the most in both.

params.title: Favorite Pets
params.description: Horizontal picture graph appears. Favorite Pets data. Dogs=5, Cats=4, Fish=3, Birds=6. Three components labeled by system overlay: A=Title 'Favorite Pets', B=Key 'Each ★ = 1 vote', C=Category labels (Dogs, Cats, Fish, Birds). Labels are clickable.

steps.text: Every part of a graph has a job. This picture graph has three parts labeled A, B, and C. Click on the part that shows what each symbol means.
Which part tells you what each symbol means?

validator.description: Student clicked component B — the key — correct

params.description: Component B (the key) highlights with glow effect

steps.text: The key tells you what each symbol means. Here, each star equals 1 vote. Title tells what the graph is about. Labels name the categories. Key shows the scale. Every part has a job.

elements.description: Horizontal picture graph. Favorite Pets data. Component B (key) highlighted showing 'Each ★ = 1 vote'.
Horizontal picture graph. Favorite Pets data. Three labeled components visible: A=Title, B=Key, C=Category labels. click_component tool active.

steps.text: Let's think about what bar graphs show. They count things in different categories so you can compare them. Which scenarios count and compare?

steps.text: Some of your picks are right. Those scenarios do count and compare categories. Check your other selections. Do they count things in categories?

incorrects.description: Student answered incorrectly. System models the correct answer.

steps.text: Let me show you how this works. Bar graphs count things in different categories. Field trips: you count votes for each destination. Library books: you count checkouts for each genre. Cafeteria: you count how many students chose each lunch. All three count and compare, so all three could use a bar graph. The answer is all of them.
You saw two kinds of comparison questions today. To answer both questions, what do you need to do?
To answer both questions, what do you need to do?

validator.description: Student answered subtract — correct, both 'how many more' and 'how many fewer' require subtraction

steps.text: 'How many more' and 'how many fewer' — both mean subtract. You find the difference to compare.

params.description: Student's Warmup graph reappears on left — horizontal picture graph with 3 categories they created earlier
A Lesson graph from earlier sections appears on right — horizontal picture graph showing data they read during the lesson

steps.text: You made your own graph. You read other people's graphs. You answered comparison questions and found totals. You know how to collect and represent data.
Next time, you'll see graphs where each symbol means more than one. Get ready for a new challenge.
